subpractice_description_raw: MMconv: Conventional Maize Monoculture (MMConv). This maize pro-duction system was designed to maximize the gross margin,according to the conventional system practiced in the South-West of France. In order to avoid water, nitrogen andmacronutrients stresses, inputs were not limited and main agri-cultural operations consisted of a spring mouldboard ploughingand a soil maintained bare during the fallow period in winter.Chemical control of weeds (preventative and curative) was usedas commonly practiced for conventional maize production. Thissystem is used as the reference to measure the relative perfor-mance of the three LI-CS.

econ_inputs: Operation cost (Moldboard ploughing, Strip-till, Field cultivator, Rottary harrow, Cultipacker, Disking, Sowing, Row crop cultivator, Rotary hoe, Broadcast fertilization application, Pesticide spraying (including molluscicide),Harvesting, Irrigation), seed, pesticides, Fertilizers
out_subindicator_description_raw: The variable costs of a cropping system in a given year are thesum of the costs of pesticides and adjuvants, fertilizers, and seedsfor both crop and cover crop. Fuel and energy costs were esti-mated for each management operation (Supplementary material2), excluding irrigation since the irrigation material was specificto the experiment and cannot be extrapolated to other situations. Gross income, an easily understandable and interpreted static,was calculated by multiplying the maize grain harvested atstandard moisture content by its price. The prices were esti-mated as the average price in the harvest month at the Chicagocommodity market, consulted on the French national statisticalinstitute (INSEEInstitut National de la Statistique et des EtudesEconomiques) (Bechini and Castoldi, 2009).

subpractice_description_raw: MMLI: Low-Input Maize Monoculture (MMLI). A low pesticides, nitro-gen (N) fertilizer and irrigation water monoculture aimed atreducing the use of fertilizer-N by 25%, herbicides by 50% (thanksto substitution with mechanical weeding and localized spraying)and irrigation water by 25% (using an half early variety). The aimwas to avoid water pollution by reducing nitrate and pesticideleaching by 50 and 70%, respectively. Soil and water protectionwas reinforced by using a cover crop (Lolium hybridum  Trifolium spp.) during the fallow period, buried by ploughing. Finally, thissystem aimed at maintaining the same gross margin as MMConv.

subpractice_description_raw: MMct: Conservation Tillage Maize Monoculture (MMCT). The mainobjective of this system was to reduce energy consumption andGHG emissions by 40%. The objective was also to reduce pesticideleaching by 50%. To reach these objectives, conservation tillagepractices were implemented and maize was either sown afterstrip tillage (2011 and 2012) or directly in no-tillage manage-ment (2013) and a cover crop was sown immediately after maizeharvest. In this system, weeds were chemically controlled withthe objective to maintain the use of herbicides at the same levelas MMConv. A slight gross margin reduction of 10% was acceptedin regard to MMConvas a compensation of human labour reduc-tion.

site_id01: The experimental trial was located in Sara Ana (390 m a.s.l.), Alto Beni, in the department of La Paz, Bolivia.
subpractice_description_raw: Mc: monoculture conventional.  In both the full-sun monocultures and agroforestry systems, plantain was also planted at a density of 625 trees ha?1.  They were removed at the end of 2011 in the full-sun monocultures and replaced by banana trees in the agroforestry systems, according to local farmers practices.
out_exp_design: Each production system was replicated four times in a completely randomized block design.
econ_inputs: prunning, Weeding, fertilization, harvest, pesticide application, labor to open, remove the beans per pod, to turn the beans during the fermentation and drying process, and to select the beans according to first and second quality, labor preparing the chipa, transportation from the field to the research station where they were sold
out_subindicator_description_raw: Revenues derived from cacao were calculated for each  year, using the total dry yield per plot and annual average sales prices of each category of beans. The average weight of bunches necessary for a chipa was estimated }to calculate the revenues from the harvested bunches. Both the organic plantains and bananas were sold as conventional because of the lack of an organic market. Costs: Pruning, weeding, and pest control
data_location: yield from figure 2 (Cacao 2014); economic outputs from figure 3 a and b

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri. Norther zone
subpractice_description_raw: conventional Norther zone: During 1993 to 2003, the field was conventionally managed with annual tillage, uniform fertilizer and herbicide rates, no cover crops and a 2-year crop rotation with corn in odd years and soybean in even years (Table 1). econ_inputs: nitrogen, other fertilizers, tillage, pesticides,seed, harvest, shredding
out_subindicator_description_raw: Profit, or return to land and management, was calculated for each 10 m grid cell each year during 1993 to 2014 by summing up all variable input costs and subtracting them from the gross return.

site_id01: ZIway
subpractice_description_raw: Control: maize with recommended fertilizer rates
out_exp_design: Study conducted at three sites over 2 years (one cropping season per year), with three farmers per site each hosting one experimental block. So N = 3 farmers per study site x 3 subplots where yield was harvested x 2 years = 18
econ_inputs: seed, fertiliser, labour
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields were obtained through field measurements, while information on seeds, fertilisers and labour inputs were obtained through farmer interviews and field observations with costs obtained from local markets. The average of three subplots was taken and used to compute the yield per ha for each farm.

site_id01: Para State
subpractice_description_raw: CAE: The main products of these regularly spaced agroforestry plantations are coconut (Cocos nucifera),  acai (Euterpe oleracea), cacao (Theobroma cacao), cupuassu (Theobroma grandiflorum), and black pepper (Piper nigrum). We directly compare two types of commercial agroforestry plantations that are ecologically similar but differ socioeconomically by distinguishing into: (a) Purely commercial operations: Commercial agroforestry enterprise (CAE) Large-scale plantations mainly by the pioneer Japanese immigrants (Yamada and Gholz 2002);

subpractice_description_raw: TTM: traditional taungya method is similar to the traditional taungya, in which the farmers slash all the vegetation on the site. subpractice_description_raw: Cereals farm 1: onion - wheat; wheat, wheat
econ_inputs: seeds, organic and inorganic fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation water, land leasing, hired labour charges, fuel, machinery needed for all field operations
out_subindicator_description_raw: All input prices and the farm gate price of each type of produce sold were recorded. Costs of manure and irrigation water were calculated based on current prices at the farm gate. All expenses and revenues were computed on a hectare basis and converted from the local currency Afghani to US$ at a rate of 50:1. Depreciation of irrigation equipment as well as interest on capital for the variable expenses incurred by the farmers during the crop season was not taken into account in our calculations of production costs. Furthermore the establishment costs of the vineyard plantations were considered amortized over their past 40 years life span. Therefore, no further amortization costs were considered.
data_location: economic outcomes from table 4

site_id01: in Tangail Forest Division in Bangladesh. field visits were made to the Banstoil and Dholapara Ranges of the Tangail Forest Division.
subpractice_description_raw: Strip plantation
econ_inputs: raising seedlings, planting cost, maintenance cost, vacancy filling, tree marking
out_subindicator_description_raw: Fifty-two respondents were randomly selected from the two forest ranges (17 participants from the strip plantation, 17 from the agroforestry plantation and 18 from the woodlot plantation). A semi-structured questionnaire was used. Interaction with interviewees was generally informal, and included two-way communication. Annual plantation raising and management costs were obtained from the Tangail Divisional Forest office. As there was no record of plantation-specific staff salary and administrative costs, yearly costs for the whole forest range were divided by the total area of the range to arrive at a per hectare cost and plantation-specific costs were then derived from this. Land tax was calculated from the official rate fixed by the land administration authority of the country.

site_id01: The experiments were conducted at Ginninderra Experiment Station (GES) near Canberra (149°060E, 35°120S)
subpractice_description_raw: Grain only (@$80/t)
econ_inputs: canola costs
out_subindicator_description_raw: Gross margin analyses were calculated for grain only and dualpurpose canola using the data for the winter canola variety Maxol, which was included in all of the experiments reported. We valued the grazed canola forage in each experiment in two-ways. First, a conservative estimate of the value of the forage was estimated assuming an agistment value of $0.50/ sheep.week, which if applied in Expt 4, would value the forage at around $80/t. Secondly, a higher value for the forage was calculated assuming a sheep meat enterprise and by applying the feed conversion and liveweight gain measured in Expt 4, a price for sheep of $A1.65/kg of liveweight gain, and a variable production cost per sheep of $A12/year. The variable production cost was converted to $27/ha for the conditions in Expt 4 (i.e. 9 sheep at $12 per year for 24 days on 0.275 ha). For the canola seed production, we assumed that canola had a variable production cost of $A350/ha and the seed was valued at $A325/t in 200405 and $A400/t in 2006.}
data_location: yield and gross margin from table 7

site_id01: This research was conducted from 1992 through 1994 in Huron County, Michigan.

econ_inputs: fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system.
out_subindicator_description_raw: The net income of each cropping system was calculated by subtracting selected variable costs from gross income. Selected variable costs included the cost of red clover seed, N fertilizer, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system. Gross income was calculated by multiplying corn grain yield from each treatment by the 1993 government target price for corn grain ($2.50). The costs of N fertilizer and fertilizer application were obtained from the Huron County, MICHIGAN, Cooperative Extension Service. Hauling and drying costs are not included. Depreciation cost was calculated by dividing the $3OO/acre cost of installing the SI system by the 20 yr expected life of the system. Installation, maintenance, and operation costs, and expected life of the SI system are fiom Belcher (1992).
data_location: gross and net income from table 8

subpractice_description_raw: NTM: continuous no-till maize
econ_inputs: seed, fertilizer, herbicide, transport, equipment, labor cost (lay out residues, plant primary crop, fertilizer application, herbicide application, hadn-weeding, harvest primary crop)
out_subindicator_description_raw: Labor costs were based on Malawi's official minimum daily wage and converted from the Malawi kwacha to US dollars. The minimum daily wage was about $1.00 per 8-h day ($1.02, $0.98, and $1.07, in years 1, 2 and 3, respectively). Market and market analogue prices were based on input and output price surveys conducted at agricultural retailers and markets in the communities near research plots as well as from markets and wholesale distributers in Lilongwe, the largest regional city and the capital of Malawi. Output price surveys for storable crops took place at harvest, 2 months after harvest, and in November, when most smallholder farmers sell any remaining produce in order to purchase inputs for the subsequent season. These prices were used  irectly for products sold and as opportunity cost based inferred values for output eaten or retained for seed.
data_location: yield from 5. economic output from table 5

site_id01: The experimental trial was located in Sara Ana (390 m a.s.l.), Alto Beni, in the department of La Paz, Bolivia.
subpractice_description_raw: Mc: monoculture conventional.  In both the full-sun monocultures and agroforestry systems, plantain was also planted at a density of 625 trees ha?1.  They were removed at the end of 2011 in the full-sun monocultures and replaced by banana trees in the agroforestry systems, according to local farmers practices.
out_exp_design: Each production system was replicated four times in a completely randomized block design.
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: Revenues derived from cacao were calculated for each  year, using the total dry yield per plot and annual average sales prices of each category of beans. The average weight of bunches necessary for a chipa was estimated }to calculate the revenues from the harvested bunches. Both the organic plantains and bananas were sold as conventional because of the lack of an organic market. Costs: Pruning, weeding, and pest control
data_location: yield from figure 2 (Cacao 2014); economic outputs from figure 3 a and b

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri
subpractice_description_raw: conventional: During 1993 to 2003, the field was conventionally managed with annual tillage, uniform fertilizer and herbicide rates, no cover crops and a 2-year crop rotation with corn in odd years and soybean in even years (Table 1). One exception to the crop rotation was sorghum instead of corn in 1995 due to extremely wet soil conditions in the spring that prevented corn planting.
econ_inputs: nitrogen, other fertilizers, tillage, pesticides,seed, harvest, shredding
out_subindicator_description_raw: Annual prices for inputs and outputs during 2007 to 2014 were considered in this analysis. Output prices for grain crops were obtained from the Center for Farm Financial Management (2018) for up to 2000 farms in nine Midwest states including Missouri. Average yields from replicated large plots adjacent to the field (Yost et al. 2016) with consistent management over time were utilized.
data_location: cost from table 3

site_id01: ZIway
subpractice_description_raw: Control: maize with recommended fertilizer rates
out_exp_design: Study conducted at three sites over 2 years (one cropping season per year), with three farmers per site each hosting one experimental block. So N = 3 farmers per study site x 3 subplots where yield was harvested x 2 years = 18
econ_inputs: seed, fertiliser, labour
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields were obtained through field measurements, while information on seeds, fertilisers and labour inputs were obtained through farmer interviews and field observations with costs obtained from local markets. Total revenue was based on sum of yields of maize, stover and legume biomass. Maize yields were based on harvesting crops from 70 sq m farm section covering three random 5.63 sq m of each subplot. The average of three subplots was taken and used to compute the yield per ha for each farm.

site_id01: The present study was carried out in the villages of Govind Wildlife Sanctuary of hilly state Uttarakhand, Western Himalaya. Three villages, such as Jakhol, Sidiri, and Saoud, were selected after preliminary survey of the wildlife Sanctuary
subpractice_description_raw: Oryza sativa
out_exp_design: Three plots of each crop were randomly selected and all the inputs and outputs were quantified.
product_raw: economic yield, crop by product, green grasses
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: survey. All information was collected through informal discussions and individual interviews held during each cropping season for three successive years with knowledgeable members of the families particularly the women folk. site_id01: This study gathered data in the communities of Agrovilla de Araipa´ and Sao Tome´. Both communities are located in the municipality of Aveiro, in the midregion of the Rio Tapajo´s in the state of Para´, Brazil.
subpractice_description_raw: BS: the alternative short-cycle crop system called Bragantino system (BS), as in the eponym region of the state of Para´, Brazil. The Bragantino system is an alternative system to slash-and-burn traditional practices developed by Carvo et al. (2005) from the Empresa Brasileira de Pesquisa Agropecua´ria (EMBRAPA). This system was designed to facilitate soil recovery after degradation. It offers a continuous production by an association of annual crops and the use of fertilizers. Those crops are cassava, beans, rice and corn. The PLUPH project used this design, but excluded the rice. The spacing between plants is lower than the spacing normally used by local farmers.
econ_inputs: maintenance, harvesting, handling, transportation
out_subindicator_description_raw: The six heads of household responsible for managing the experimental systems were interviewed concerning their perspectives about sustainable forms of agricultural production, local market conditions, availability of labour, equipment needed for production, and transport costs. Additional economic data not known by farmers was sourced from the EMBRAPA and from academic literature.
data_location: Economic output from table 3

econ_inputs: seeds, organic and inorganic fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation water, land leasing, hired labour charges, fuel, machinery needed for all field operations
out_subindicator_description_raw: All input prices and the farm gate price of each type of produce sold were recorded. Costs of manure and irrigation water were calculated based on current prices at the farm gate. All expenses and revenues were computed on a hectare basis and converted from the local currency Afghani to US$ at a rate of 50:1. Depreciation of irrigation equipment as well as interest on capital for the variable expenses incurred by the farmers during the crop season was not taken into account in our calculations of production costs. Furthermore the establishment costs of the vineyard plantations were considered amortized over their past 40 years life span. Therefore, no further amortization costs were considered.
data_location: economic outcomes from table 4

site_id01: A field experiment was conducted between July until December 2017 at Mungka, Lima Puluh Kota Regency, West Sumatra province in Indonesia.
subpractice_description_raw: CON:Conventional rice system
out_exp_design: The experimental design was a Randomised Complete Block Design with six treatments and three replicate blocks.
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic performance of each treatment was assessed from the input costs and the revenue of the harvested products of one rice cropping cycle. Besides producing rice, the treatments ORB, ORF, ORD, and CRS also produced long beans, fish and/or ducks. The investment and potential revenue of these marketable products were included in the economic calculations. Input costs consisted of costs for fertilisers (kg), pesticides (kg), compost (kg), bio-pesticides (packet), plant seeds (kg), long bean seeds (kg), ducks, fish, duck foods (kg), fish foods (kg) and labour (hours). The costs of labour were determined from the actual labour input in the experiment and were calculated based on farm wage per hour in Indonesian Rupiah (IDR). The revenue from the harvested rice (kg), long beans (kg), fish (kg), and ducks (birds) was calculated based on production and prices. The price was determined by the market price for the product at the time of the experiment. The gross margin per treatment was  calculated as the difference between the revenue and costs. Finally,
data_location: yield from table 3. Economic output from table 4. Labor input from figure 2

econ_inputs: labor (preparation of the treatments [soil preparation, rice seedlings and transplanting]; management [application chemical fertiliser, spraying pesticides and hand weeding], harvesting [rice]), fertilisers (kg), pesticides (kg), plant seeds (kg).
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic performance of each treatment was assessed from the input costs and the revenue of the harvested products of one rice cropping cycle. Besides producing rice, the treatments ORB, ORF, ORD, and CRS also produced long beans, fish and/or ducks. The investment and potential revenue of these marketable products were included in the economic calculations. Input costs consisted of costs for fertilisers (kg), pesticides (kg), compost (kg), bio-pesticides (packet), plant seeds (kg), long bean seeds (kg), ducks, fish, duck foods (kg), fish foods (kg) and labour (hours). The costs of labour were determined from the actual labour input in the experiment and were calculated based on farm wage per hour in Indonesian Rupiah (IDR). The revenue from the harvested rice (kg), long beans (kg), fish (kg), and ducks (birds) was calculated based on production and prices. The price was determined by the market price for the product at the time of the experiment. The gross margin per treatment was  calculated as the difference between the revenue and costs. Finally,
data_location: yield from table 3. Economic output from table 4. Labor input from figure 2

econ_inputs: wheat seed, fertilizer, tillage, pesticide
out_subindicator_description_raw: Costs of production and net returns were determined annually for each cropping system. All purchased inputs and machine operations were valued and held constant at their 2001 cost levels (Saskatchewan Agriculture and Food 2001; University of Saskatchewan 2001). Participation in the Canada/Saskatchewan Crop Insurance Program was assumed to be at the 70% yield coverage for wheat. Premium rates and payout criteria for Risk Area #10 of Saskatchewan were assumed (Saskatchewan Crop Insurance Corporation 2001). The research plot data were extrapolated to the farm-level using a 907-ha representative farm with a typical complement of machinery and labor supply for each treatment.

chem_subpractice_raw: pre-plant-incorporated herbicide application of 2.3 kg·ha1 N,N- diethyl-2-(1-naphthalenyloxy) propanamide (napropamide) and 4.7 kg·ha1 S-propyl butylethylcarbamothioate (pebulate), and with postemergence applications of 0.56 kg·ha1 4-amino-6-(1,1,dimethylethyl)-3-(methylthio)-1,2,4-triazin-5(4H)-one (metribuzin) and 0.2 kg·ha12-[l-(ethoxyimino)butyl]-5-[2-(ethylthio)propyl]-3-hydroxy-2-cyclohexen-1-one (sethoxydim).
out_exp_design: The sample size for each treatment is the total number of replications in all 3 years, and is 28 for the bare soil and hairy vetch systems and 24 for the black polyethylene system.
econ_inputs: bed preparation, totato seedling, labor, fertilizers, postemergence herbicides, labor, pesticides, labor, stakingtiening, labor, irrigation, machinery costs, harvest, labor, marketing, postharvest labor, interest
out_subindicator_description_raw: Gross returns are calculated in this paper by matching the actual weekly harvested tomatoes with the appropriate weekly price summed over the total number of harvests for each of the 3 years.
data_location: yield and economic outcomes from tables 3, 4, 5 (experimental plots)

site_id01: This research was conducted from 1992 through 1994 in Huron County, Michigan.
subpractice_description_raw: No green manure system (0)
out_exp_design: 4 replications per treatment x 2 years = 8 sample size
econ_inputs: fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system.
out_subindicator_description_raw: The net income of each cropping system was calculated by subtracting selected variable costs from gross income. Selected variable costs included the cost of red clover seed, N fertilizer, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system. Gross income was calculated by multiplying corn grain yield from each treatment by the 1993 government target price for corn grain ($2.50). The costs of N fertilizer and fertilizer application were obtained from the Huron County, MICHIGAN, Cooperative Extension Service. Hauling and drying costs are not included. Depreciation cost was calculated by dividing the $3OO/acre cost of installing the SI system by the 20 yr expected life of the system. Installation, maintenance, and operation costs, and expected life of the SI system are fiom Belcher (1992).
data_location: gross and net income from table 8

site_id01: the Northwest Agricultural Research Station of the Ohio Agricultural Research and Development Center (OARDC) in Custar, OH(40°13?0.8?N, 83°45?53.9?).
subpractice_description_raw: Wheat NR  DC soybean: inter wheat grown in NR followed by DC soybean
out_exp_design: four replications of treatments x 3 years = 12 sample size
econ_inputs: seed, N fertilizer, N application, herbicide, herbicide aplication, planting, harvesting
out_subindicator_description_raw: Cropping system profitability was estimated by subtracting costs that varied across treatments from gross revenue. Gross revenue was calculated by multiplying soybean, wheat, and forage yield from the field experiment by prevailing prices in 2016, 2017, and 2018 (Sulc, 2019; USDA-NASS, 2020) (Table 2). Costs that varied across treatments included seed, N fertilizer product and application cost, herbicide product and application cost, planting, and harvesting. Costs were additive for each crop in the system.
data_location: yield from table 4, profitability from table 5

subpractice_description_raw: Beef cattle production with soy leasing (BSL): beef cattle production with leasing land for soybean cultivation. BSL is an ICLS with a full-cycle beef cattle production and soybean crop in the summer through a land lease, to be undertaken by an external farmer. The lease payment is fixed (Table 2) in tonnes soybean/ha for the use of 250 ha during the period of preparation, cultivation and harvest of soybean (October and May).
out_exp_design: The economic evaluation was based on gross margin per hectare, calculated as the difference between the revenue generated for each activity and production costs.

out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic evaluation was based on gross margin per hectare, calculated as the difference between the revenue generated for each activity and production costs.

site_id01: The experiment was started in the spring of 2011 at the Domainede LamotheINP-EI Purpan, Seysses, Garonne Plain, south-westernFrance (43.506N, 1.237E).
subpractice_description_raw: Maize-MSW: Integrated Maize Rotation (Maize-MSW). This system included athree-year rotation of maize-soybean-winter soft wheat and wasdesigned to reduce, at the rotation level, the inputs of herbicide,irrigation water and fertilizer-N by 50% compared to MMConv.Results from only the maize phase of the rotation are presentedhere. The maize prioritized the same input reduction objectivesas MMLI, reducing the use of herbicides by 50% and the use offertilizer-N and irrigation water by 25%. As for MMLI, the objec-tive was to reduce nitrate and pesticide leaching by 50 and 70%,respectively. However, Maize-MSW aimed at maintaining thesame gross margin as MMConv.
chem_subpractice_raw: S-Metolachlor, Glyphosate, Thiametoxam, Nicosulfuron, Mesotrione, 2.4-MCPA, Metaldehyde
econ_inputs: Operation cost (Moldboard ploughing, Strip-till, Field cultivator, Rottary harrow, Cultipacker, Disking, Sowing, Row crop cultivator, Rotary hoe, Broadcast fertilization application, Pesticide spraying (including molluscicide),Harvesting, Irrigation), seed, pesticides, Fertilizers, Energy
out_subindicator_description_raw: The variable costs of a cropping system in a given year are thesum of the costs of pesticides and adjuvants, fertilizers, and seedsfor both crop and cover crop. Fuel and energy costs were esti-mated for each management operation (Supplementary material2), excluding irrigation since the irrigation material was specificto the experiment and cannot be extrapolated to other situations. Gross income, an easily understandable and interpreted static,was calculated by multiplying the maize grain harvested atstandard moisture content by its price. The prices were esti-mated as the average price in the harvest month at the Chicagocommodity market, consulted on the French national statisticalinstitute (INSEEInstitut National de la Statistique et des EtudesEconomiques) (Bechini and Castoldi, 2009).
data_location: crop yield from table 3, fertilizer use, labor, energy from table 5. gross margin from table 5. input list from supp info. the averageexchange rate USD-D in the harvest month, given by on INSEE,was used to convert the commodity price into  (no exchange rate data in the paper)

out_subindicator_description_raw: Labor costs were based on Malawi's official minimum daily wage and converted from the Malawi kwacha to US dollars. The minimum daily wage was about $1.00 per 8-h day ($1.02, $0.98, and $1.07, in years 1, 2 and 3, respectively). Market and market analogue prices were based on input and output price surveys conducted at agricultural retailers and markets in the communities near research plots as well as from markets and wholesale distributers in Lilongwe, the largest regional city and the capital of Malawi. Output price surveys for storable crops took place at harvest, 2 months after harvest, and in November, when most smallholder farmers sell any remaining produce in order to purchase inputs for the subsequent season. These prices were used  irectly for products sold and as opportunity cost based inferred values for output eaten or retained for seed.

site_id01: The trials were carried out from 2005 to 2009 at Tomejil farm, which is located in Carmona, Seville, Spain (37º24'07"N 5º35'10"0).
subpractice_description_raw: organic rotation was wheatsunflowerpeasfaba bean (as green manure), with each crop covering 1 ha (total 4 ha). The
product_raw: crop yield (wheat, sunflower, peas, faba bean) CAP payments, subsidies for organic production.
econ_inputs: wheat (disk harrow tillage, disk harrow tillage, cultivator tillage, wheat seed, wheat sowing, harvesting), sunflower (chisel tillage, cultivator tillage, vibro-cultivator, sunflower seed, sunflower sowing, cultivator tillage inter-rows, harvesting), peas (disc harrow tillage, cultivator tillage, vibro-cultivator, pe seeds, pea sowing, harvesting), faba bea(disc harrow tillage, cultivator tillage, faba bean seed, faba bean sowing, disc harrow tillage)
out_subindicator_description_raw: For the economic analysis, we separate the operational costs, income and net margin for the two cropping systems. The purpose was to allow correct understanding of the relevance of each component to final farm profitability. For the organic system, margins were calculated using prices for the conventional system in order to assess how premium prices influence the final profitability of the farm.
data_location: yield from table 2. Inputs from table 1. economic outputs from table 4, 5

site_id01: UGA Southeast Georgia Research and Education Center at Midville, GA (Burke County, 32°52'15.6"N 82°13'12.0"W).
subpractice_description_raw: Crimson clover: Cotton  cover crops were planted early November using a cultipacker or grain drill and chemically terminated and rolled using a straight bar roller crimper 14 days prior to cotton planting
chem_subpractice_raw: Grmoxone, Reflex, Diuron, Folex, Drop, Ethephon, Glyphosate, MSMA, Diuron,  COC, Envoke
out_exp_design: The experimental design consisted of 0.4 ha plots (roughly square) organized in a completely randomized block design (n=4/treatment) for a total of 12 plots each sample year.
econ_inputs: planting cover crops, cultivating the land, planting cotton, herbicide applications, harvesting and ginning of cotton, herbicide cost, fuel, lubricants, repairs, maintenance, labor, electricity, depreciation (depreciation is both time-related and use related), overhead costs (interest, insurance, and housing).
out_subindicator_description_raw: To estimate the economic benefits of adopting cover crops in cotton production, the net return per acre was compared for each of the three treatments of cover crops (i) for 2017 and 2018 (t). The gross revenue was calculated using the lint and cottonseed yields and their historical prices each year.
data_location: yield from table 1 (2018-lint yield). Economic outputs from table C2 in supp info (I divided by 0.404686 to transfor USD/acre to USD/ha

site_id01: University of california Desert station in coachella valley
subpractice_description_raw: Cowpea mulch ICM: Integrated crop system (low-input system) where pesticide was only applied when insect populations reached economic threshold levels, however fertilizer used (15N-15P-15K) was the same as in conventional treatments. Summer cover crop cowpea used as mulch in the fall
out_exp_design: Yields measured in-field using a block design with 4 replicates and 2 year of study, so N=8. Total variable costs include production and harvest costs, and production costs were based on standard desert conventional production costs adjusted to reflect actual costs for specific cover crops and mangement practices (but it implies the same costs were used for all replicates in a treatment so N=2) while harvest costs were adjusted to reflect variations in crop yield in each year (implying different costs were used per plot so N=8).
econ_inputs: production cost (border, cross check and breatk, fertilizer cost of application, transplant, install drip and repair, spike, hand weed, irrigation, insect control, land rent, cash overhead) ,harvesting
out_subindicator_description_raw: gross return was calculated as average price of $8/box (15.9kg) multiply by average boxes/ha for lettuce and $7.7/box (18kg) for cantaloupe. See Table 3 and 5 for breakdown of production costs. Harvest costs were adjusted based on number of boxes harvested per treatment multiplied by picking cost.
data_location: Economic output and yield from table 4 coordinates obtained from google

site_id01: The experimental trial was located in Sara Ana (390 m a.s.l.), Alto Beni, in the department of La Paz, Bolivia.
subpractice_description_raw: Afc: agroforestry conventional. the main shade trees were Inga spp. and Erythrina spp., complemented by timber, fruit, and }palm trees. In both the full-sun monocultures and agroforestry systems, plantain was also planted at a density of 625 trees ha?1. They were removed at the end of 2011 in the full-sun monocultures and replaced by banana trees in the agroforestry systems, according to local farmers practices.
out_exp_design: Each production system was replicated four times in a completely randomized block design.
econ_inputs: prunning, Weeding, fertilization, harvest, pesticide application, labor to open, remove the beans per pod, to turn the beans during the fermentation and drying process, and to select the beans according to first and second quality, labor preparing the chipa, transportation from the field to the research station where they were sold
out_subindicator_description_raw: Revenues derived from cacao were calculated for each  year, using the total dry yield per plot and annual average sales prices of each category of beans. The average weight of bunches necessary for a chipa was estimated }to calculate the revenues from the harvested bunches. Both the organic plantains and bananas were sold as conventional because of the lack of an organic market. The potential future revenues of the timber trees in the agroforestry systems were not considered because it was too early to estimate their potential growth at this stage and because we aimed to show the actual figures regarding the costs and benefits in a young cacao plantation. Costs: Pruning, weeding, and pest control
data_location: yield from figure 2 (Cacao 2014); economic outputs from figure 3 a and b

site_id01: The experimental trial was located in Sara Ana (390 m a.s.l.), Alto Beni, in the department of La Paz, Bolivia.
subpractice_description_raw: Afo: agroforestry organic. the main shade trees were Inga spp. and Erythrina spp., complemented by timber, fruit, and palm trees.  In both the full-sun monocultures and agroforestry systems, plantain was also planted at a density of 625 trees ha?1.  They were removed at the end of 2011 in the full-sun monocultures and replaced by banana trees in the agroforestry systems, according to local farmers practices. A perennial legume cover crop (Neonotonia wightii (Arn.) Lackey) was sown in the organically managed systems
out_exp_design: Each production system was replicated four times in a completely randomized block design.
econ_inputs: prunning, Weeding, fertilization, harvest, pesticide application, labor to open, remove the beans per pod, to turn the beans during the fermentation and drying process, and to select the beans according to first and second quality, labor preparing the chipa, transportation from the field to the research station where they were sold
out_subindicator_description_raw: Revenues derived from cacao were calculated for each  year, using the total dry yield per plot and annual average sales prices of each category of beans. The average weight of bunches necessary for a chipa was estimated }to calculate the revenues from the harvested bunches. Both the organic plantains and bananas were sold as conventional because of the lack of an organic market. The potential future revenues of the timber trees in the agroforestry systems were not considered because it was too early to estimate their potential growth at this stage and because we aimed to show the actual figures regarding the costs and benefits in a young cacao plantation. Costs: Pruning, weeding, and pest control
data_location: yield from figure 2 (Cacao 2014); economic outputs from figure 3 a and b

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri. Norther zone
subpractice_description_raw: PAS Norther zone: The PAS system was implemented during 2004 to 2014. Management practices used across the entire field included: (i) no-tillage; (ii) cover crops in all years; (iii) variable-rate N fertilizer applied to cereal grain crops using commercial ground-based canopy reflectance technologies (iv) zonal or variable-rate P, K and lime fertilizer based on 30-m gridsample soil-test results and University of Missouri fertilizer recommendations. In this zone, winter wheat replaced corn in PAS. Cover crops following wheat included medium red clover (Trifolium pratense L.), sudangrass (Sorghum sudanense P. Stapf) or mixtures of legumes and non-legumes.
chem_subpractice_raw: atrazine, other post-emerge plant-active herbicides, glyphosate
econ_inputs: cover crops, nitrogen, other fertilizers, tillage, pesticides,seed, harvest, shredding
out_subindicator_description_raw: Profit, or return to land and management, was calculated for each 10 m grid cell each year during 1993 to 2014 by summing up all variable input costs and subtracting them from the gross return.
data_location: profitability from table 4 (conventional 1993-2003; PAS 2004-2014)

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri. South zone
subpractice_description_raw: PAS South: The PAS system was implemented during 2004 to 2014. Management practices used across the entire field included: (i) no-tillage; (ii) cover crops in all years; (iii) variable-rate N fertilizer applied to cereal grain crops using commercial ground-based canopy reflectance technologies (iv) zonal or variable-rate P, K and lime fertilizer based on 30-m gridsample soil-test results and University of Missouri fertilizer recommendations. Cover crops following corn included cereal rye (Secale cereals L.) or mixtures of legumes and nonlegumes and covers following soybean included annual ryegrass (Lolium multiflorum Lam.) or mixtures of legumes and nonlegumes.

site_id01: ZIway
subpractice_description_raw: MD  SH: maize intercropped with sunnhemp and microdosed fertilizer
out_exp_design: Study conducted at three sites over 2 years (one cropping season per year), with three farmers per site each hosting one experimental block. So N = 3 farmers per study site x 3 subplots where yield was harvested x 2 years = 18
econ_inputs: seed, fertiliser, labour
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields were obtained through field measurements, while information on seeds, fertilisers and labour inputs were obtained through farmer interviews and field observations with costs obtained from local markets. Total revenue was based on sum of yields of maize, stover and legume biomass. Maize yields were based on harvesting crops from 70 sq m farm section covering three random 5.63 sq m of each subplot. The average of three subplots was taken and used to compute the yield per ha for each farm.
data_location: Table A1

site_id01: Maranhao state
subpractice_description_raw: EF: Enriched fallow (EF) This agroforestry system is established by enrichment plantings of fruit and timber in the understory of old secondary forests. The degree of human control over vegetation structure and species composition is far lower than in all other systems covered by our study. Fallow enrichment developed independently in various site clusters by isolated initiatives of innovative farmers partly was initiated (and thereafter abandoned) by a local NGO. Enrichment plantings are an ecologically valuable option for income generation in otherwise unproductive forest reserves required by law. In our study, secondary forest age ranged from 6 to [30 years and enrichment planting age was 312 years. Banana, acai, and bacuri (Platonia insignis) are the most important enrichment species.
out_exp_design: Number of sites from figure 2 in parenthesis
econ_inputs: maintenance, harvest operations, external inputs and (own-family or hired) labor
out_subindicator_description_raw: semistructured interviews with open questions
data_location: the systems were classified as simplified or diversified according table 1 (number of species). Economic output from figure 4

site_id01: province of Guayas
subpractice_description_raw: Organic:  Farms with organic management are agroforestry systems with a high degree of diversification and no use of synthetic compounds, such as fertilizers or pesticides, and which have opted for the following: (1) technified irrigation and/or (2) the ecological and cultural intensification of management (high doses of organic fertilization, mechanic undergrowth control, cob collection, regular pruning for maintenance and formation, and the use of improved national varieties) with the purpose of also improving their yield per hectare.

site_id01: This study was conducted in two villages (villages P and T) in Matupi Township, southern Chin State, Myanmar.
subpractice_description_raw: CcTM: The CcTM is introduced as a modification of terrace farming initiated by government program. Also in this method, the trees are kept as orchard canopy. The farmers prepare flat and narrow stretches of ground (terraces) along the contour line under the trees in old fallows or mature forests. The terraces are about 30 cm in width. The initial cost to make terraces along the contour lines is high; consequently only a few farmers (n = 9) use this method (Supplementary Table 1).
econ_inputs: land registration, terrace making along contour line, regeneration stocks (bulbils  corms), planting, weeding (one time), fencing
out_subindicator_description_raw: The cost-benefit analysis between different cultivation methods was conducted by using interview data on the initial investment cost and income from Wa-u during the beginning of their cultivation and the studied year.

econ_inputs: land lease per year, summer land prepa-ration for fallows, sowing, two intercultivation operations with theTropicultor in the rainy season, fertilizer P, ZnSO4, their application costs, cost of two hand-weedings in the rainy seasonand one hand-weeding in the post-rainy season, fertilizer N, seeds, pesticides, harvesting, threshing

site_id01: Waikerie in South Australia (348170S, 1408020E) in 1998
subpractice_description_raw: Wheat after pasture: The traditional practice consisted of two phased treatments of a cultivated pasturewheat rotation in alternate years with low sowing inputs of fertiliser (5 kg N, 11 kg phosphorus (P) ha1) and seed (45 kg ha1). In this rotation, the pasture comprises self-sown forbs and herbs (Chondrilla juncea, Raphanus raphanistrum, Heliotropium europaeum), summer-growing annual grasses (Polypogon spp., Tragus australianus) and Medicago spp. that emerge in response to rainfall. This may occur late in the cereal phase when weeds are no longer controlled with herbicides, or any time in response to rainfall events through the next autumn and winter season. Such pastures are typically grazed by sheep, although in this experiment grazing was simulated by mowing pasture two or three times during a phase and spray-topping in the months before a cereal phase. At the end of the pasture phase, typically early summer, cultivation was used to control weeds in preparation for the cereal phase, with up to four cultivations in summer and early spring (MarchApril) before sowing.
econ_inputs: pesticide, fertilizer, herbicide application, fertilizer application,
out_subindicator_description_raw: Gross margins were calculated using average costs and grain prices of each system over the duration of the experiment (19982008).
data_location: yield from figure 1. economic output from figure 3

Depreciation of irrigation equipment as well as interest on capital for the variable expenses incurred by the farmers during the crop season was not taken into account in our calculations of production costs. Furthermore the establishment costs of the vineyard plantations were considered amortized over their past 40 years life span. Therefore, no further amortization costs were considered.
data_location: economic outcomes from table 4

site_id01: Loess Plateau Research Station of Lanzhou University, located in the township of Shishe, Xifeng City, Gansu, China
subpractice_description_raw: FWVW: summer fallow-winter wheat-common vetch-winter wheat
econ_inputs: sowing (labor, seed, tillage [fuel, repair, maintenance of the equipment]), N and P fertilizers, weed management, harvest
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic analysis was conducted by calculating net income for the different rotation systems using production costs and crop prices obtained from the Chinese National Agricultural Market Service Database (http://datacenter.cngrain.com) for the experimental period (20112013) (Tables 2 and 3). The cost of sowing included labor, seed, and tillage, and the cost of tillage included fuel, repair, and maintenance of the equipment. Additional production costs included urea and triple superphosphate fertilizers, weed management, and harvest. The costs of weed management and harvest were labor. Net income of each crop rotation system was calculated as the difference between the gross income and production costs U. S. dollars.

site_id01: A field experiment was conducted between July until December 2017 at Mungka, Lima Puluh Kota Regency, West Sumatra province in Indonesia.
subpractice_description_raw: ORB:organic rice with border crops. The ORB system combined organic rice cultivation with the additional border crops sunn hemp (Crotalaria juncea) and long beans (Phaseolus vulgaris) grown on the dikes.
chem_subpractice_raw: extracts of garlic (Allium sativum) and ginger (Zingiber officinale) and wood ash and salt were used to control pests and diseases in these systems
out_exp_design: The experimental design was a Randomised Complete Block Design with six treatments and three replicate blocks.
econ_inputs: labor (preparation of the treatments [soil preparation, rice seedlings and transplanting, border crops preparation]; management [application organic fertiliser, long beans and sun hemps management], harvesting [rice, long beans]), compost (kg), bio-pesticides (packet), plant seeds (kg), long bean seeds (kg)
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic performance of each treatment was assessed from the input costs and the revenue of the harvested products of one rice cropping cycle. Besides producing rice, the treatments ORB, ORF, ORD, and CRS also produced long beans, fish and/or ducks. The investment and potential revenue of these marketable products were included in the economic calculations. Input costs consisted of costs for fertilisers (kg), pesticides (kg), compost (kg), bio-pesticides (packet), plant seeds (kg), long bean seeds (kg), ducks, fish, duck foods (kg), fish foods (kg) and labour (hours). The costs of labour were determined from the actual labour input in the experiment and were calculated based on farm wage per hour in Indonesian Rupiah (IDR). The revenue from the harvested rice (kg), long beans (kg), fish (kg), and ducks (birds) was calculated based on production and prices. The price was determined by the market price for the product at the time of the experiment. The gross margin per treatment was  calculated as the difference between the revenue and costs. Finally,
data_location: yield from table 3. Economic output from table 4. Labor input from figure 2

out_exp_design: Number of samples from table A in SI1
econ_inputs: labor (adult man, adult woman), diesel, machinery/electric motor, farm machinery, fertilizer (N, P2O2, K2O), superior chemicals (herbicide, insecticide), micronutrient and others
out_subindicator_description_raw: Field data were collected from the farm households through face-to-face interviews with a semi-structured interview schedule, standardized through its pre-testing on non-sampled respondents. The interview schedule included items on different primary (land preparation, irrigation application, sowing, harvesting, etc.) and secondary (fertilizer and pesticide application) field operations, input (seed, fertilizer, etc.) management, and economics of cultivation (cost of cultivation, gross and net returns, etc.). The cost of cultivation of any system (US$ha?1) was estimated from total expenditure to perform different field operations and input used for any system. Then the gross return, net return, and benefit:cost ratio (B:C ratio) was estimated as per Table 1.
data_location: yield from figure 2. economic output from table D (supportive information S1)

site_id01: VPI&SU's Whitethorne Research Farm in Montgomery County in summer, 1987.
subpractice_description_raw: Rye, no-till: corn and rye as cover crop
econ_inputs: fertilizer, herbicides, seed, machinery and fuel, labor
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic analysis provided another comparative measure of the performance of each corn production system. Prices for fertilizers, herbicides, fuel, and seeds were obtained from local retail suppliers. The price of labor was established from regional farm wage rate data (Virginia Agricultural Statistics Service, 1993). Retail prices for farm machinery were obtained from local suppliers, product manufacturers, and published sources (North American Equipment Dealers Assoc, 1990).  Costs for all items included in the economic analysis were subtracted from gross revenue to yield net revenue for each treatment, which was the basis for comparing economic performance. Costs associated with land rental or ownership were assumed to be constant among treatments and were not included.
data_location: economic output from table 3. Inputs from figure 3. Yield from figure 1

subpractice_description_raw: GS-S Conventional NO irrigated: grain sorghum - Soybean
econ_inputs: fertilizer, pesticide, seeds, tillage
out_subindicator_description_raw: Economic analysis was undertaken with the aid of enterprise budgeting techniques. These budgets were compiled for each plot using Mississippi State Budget Generator (MSBG) (Spurlock, 1992) to determine which cropping system and tillage practice would result in the highest net returns above total specified costs. The enterprise budget technique was chosen for its simplicity and applicability to the problem at hand (Kay and Edwards, 1994)

site_id01: in Tangail Forest Division in Bangladesh. field visits were made to the Banstoil and Dholapara Ranges of the Tangail Forest Division.
subpractice_description_raw: Agroforestry: In the agroforestry plantation, each participant was allocated 1 ha of degraded forest land where they utilized the alley cropping model. In this model, alternating space is provided for trees and cereal agricultural crops. The first 3m were for trees and three rows were planted with a spacing of 1.5_x0003_1.5 m. The next 9m were dedicated to agricultural crops. This pattern of trees and agricultural crops was repeated until the agroforestry plot was filled. An estimated 1600 trees ha_x0002_1 were planted in this manner.
econ_inputs: raising seedlings, planting cost, maintenance cost, vacancy filling, tree marking
out_subindicator_description_raw: Fifty-two respondents were randomly selected from the two forest ranges (17 participants from the strip plantation, 17 from the agroforestry plantation and 18 from the woodlot plantation). A semi-structured questionnaire was used. Interaction with interviewees was generally informal, and included two-way communication. Annual plantation raising and management costs were obtained from the Tangail Divisional Forest office. As there was no record of plantation-specific staff salary and administrative costs, yearly costs for the whole forest range were divided by the total area of the range to arrive at a per hectare cost and plantation-specific costs were then derived from this. Land tax was calculated from the official rate fixed by the land administration authority of the country.
data_location: economic output from table 8

site_id01: Data were collected from experimental plots established in the fall of 1993 in Cass County, near Fargo, North Dakota. Data collection continued through 2001.
subpractice_description_raw: RI: rotated soybean (SB) and spring wheat (SW) and sweet clover (SC)rye, employed mulch tillage (30% or greater residue)
out_exp_design: The experiment was a randomized complete block design with three replicates.
econ_inputs: seed, fertilizer, pesticides, Seeding, harvest, pesticide application costs and tillage operations
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields of every crop, except SC, were reported annually. Prices were obtained from the US Department of Agriculture (USDA) North Dakota Agricultural Statistics Service14. For SC prices, the Hay All, Baled category was used. Fertilizer prices were taken from the North Dakota projected crop budgets for 200415. All costs and prices in this study were deflated to 2001 dollars using a Producer Price Index16. Prices for N, P and K were $0.39, $0.44 and $0.27 kg-1, respectively. Herbicide prices were obtained  from Zollinger17. Seeding, harvest, pesticide application costs and tillage operations were recorded annually. Tillage costs were taken from Lazarus and Selley18.
data_location: yield (soybean) from table 1, returns from table 3

site_id01: The experiments were conducted at Ginninderra Experiment Station (GES) near Canberra (149°060E, 35°120S)
subpractice_description_raw: Graze/grain (@$80/t): canola and sheeps
econ_inputs: canola and sheep costs
out_subindicator_description_raw: Gross margin analyses were calculated for grain only and dualpurpose canola using the data for the winter canola variety Maxol, which was included in all of the experiments reported. We valued the grazed canola forage in each experiment in two-ways. First, a conservative estimate of the value of the forage was estimated assuming an agistment value of $0.50/ sheep.week, which if applied in Expt 4, would value the forage at around $80/t. Secondly, a higher value for the forage was calculated assuming a sheep meat enterprise and by applying the feed conversion and liveweight gain measured in Expt 4, a price for sheep of $A1.65/kg of liveweight gain, and a variable production cost per sheep of $A12/year. The variable production cost was converted to $27/ha for the conditions in Expt 4 (i.e. 9 sheep at $12 per year for 24 days on 0.275 ha). For the canola seed production, we assumed that canola had a variable production cost of $A350/ha and the seed was valued at $A325/t in 200405 and $A400/t in 2006.}
data_location: yield and gross margin from table 7

out_subindicator_description_raw: Prices used in the simulation model for cotton lint and ammonium nitrate were collected from 1984 to 2012 (USDA NASS, 2016) All prices were adjusted to 2012 U.S. dollars using the Implicit Gross Domestic Product Price Deflator (U .S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, 2016). Cover crop seed prices were collected from 2006 to 2012 through personal communication with the Tennessee Farmer Cooperative. Cover crop seed prices were also adjusted to 2012 dollars using the Implicit Gross Domestic Product Price Deflator (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, 2016). Machinery and labor costs were calculated using University of Tennessee Field Crop Budgets for 2015 (Smith et al., 2015). Total establishment costs for each cover crop were US$130.02 ha1 for hairy vetch, and $106.27 ha1 for winter wheat. The cost of destroying a cover crop was $47.45 ha1 for no-till and $93.17 ha1 for till (Smith et al., 2015).

out_exp_design: The experimental design was a randomized complete block with split-plots and four replications. 4 replications x 29 years = 118
econ_inputs: Machinery, chemical, cover crop, seed costs, fertilizer
out_subindicator_description_raw: Prices used in the simulation model for cotton lint and ammonium nitrate were collected from 1984 to 2012 (USDA NASS, 2016) All prices were adjusted to 2012 U.S. dollars using the Implicit Gross Domestic Product Price Deflator (U .S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, 2016). Cover crop seed prices were collected from 2006 to 2012 through personal communication with the Tennessee Farmer Cooperative. Cover crop seed prices were also adjusted to 2012 dollars using the Implicit Gross Domestic Product Price Deflator (U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, 2016). Machinery and labor costs were calculated using University of Tennessee Field Crop Budgets for 2015 (Smith et al., 2015). Total establishment costs for each cover crop were US$130.02 ha1 for hairy vetch, and $106.27 ha1 for winter wheat. The cost of destroying a cover crop was $47.45 ha1 for no-till and $93.17 ha1 for till (Smith et al., 2015).
data_location: net income and yield from table 4

site_id01: This research was conducted from 1992 through 1994 in Huron County, Michigan.
subpractice_description_raw: Red clover system (0)
out_exp_design: 4 replications per treatment x 2 years = 8 sample size
econ_inputs: cost of red clover seed, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system.
out_subindicator_description_raw: The net income of each cropping system was calculated by subtracting selected variable costs from gross income. Selected variable costs included the cost of red clover seed, N fertilizer, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system. Gross income was calculated by multiplying corn grain yield from each treatment by the 1993 government target price for corn grain ($2.50). The costs of N fertilizer and fertilizer application were obtained from the Huron County, MICHIGAN, Cooperative Extension Service. Hauling and drying costs are not included. Depreciation cost was calculated by dividing the $3OO/acre cost of installing the SI system by the 20 yr expected life of the system. Installation, maintenance, and operation costs, and expected life of the SI system are fiom Belcher (1992).
data_location: gross and net income from table 8

site_id01: Nkhotakota
subpractice_description_raw: CAR: conservation agriculture rotation (Cassava, pigeon pea, cowpea, pigeon pea, maize
econ_inputs: seed, fertilizer, herbicide, transport, equipment, labor cost (lay out residues, plant primary crop, plant intercrop, fertilizer application, herbicide application, hadn-weeding, harvest primary crop, harvest intercrop/firewood)
out_subindicator_description_raw: Labor costs were based on Malawi's official minimum daily wage and converted from the Malawi kwacha to US dollars. The minimum daily wage was about $1.00 per 8-h day ($1.02, $0.98, and $1.07, in years 1, 2 and 3, respectively). Market and market analogue prices were based on input and output price surveys conducted at agricultural retailers and markets in the communities near research plots as well as from markets and wholesale distributers in Lilongwe, the largest regional city and the capital of Malawi. Output price surveys for storable crops took place at harvest, 2 months after harvest, and in November, when most smallholder farmers sell any remaining produce in order to purchase inputs for the subsequent season. These prices were used  irectly for products sold and as opportunity cost based inferred values for output eaten or retained for seed.
data_location: yield from 5. economic output from table 5

subpractice_description_raw: Cowpea mulch Conventional: Conventional (standard managenment practice), where synthetic fertilizer 15 N-15P-15K was applied at the reate of 170 kg/ha. Summer cover crop cowpea used as mulch in the fall
out_exp_design: Yields measured in-field using a block design with 4 replicates and 2 year of study, so N=8. Total variable costs include production and harvest costs, and production costs were based on standard desert conventional production costs adjusted to reflect actual costs for specific cover crops and mangement practices (but it implies the same costs were used for all replicates in a treatment so N=2) while harvest costs were adjusted to reflect variations in crop yield in each year (implying different costs were used per plot so N=8).
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: gross return was calculated as average price of $8/box (15.9kg) multiply by average boxes/ha for lettuce and $7.7/box (18kg) for cantaloupe. See Table 3 and 5 for breakdown of production costs. Harvest costs were adjusted based on number of boxes harvested per treatment multiplied by picking cost.
data_location: Economic output and yield from table 4 coordinates obtained from google

out_exp_design: Yields measured in-field using a block design with 4 replicates and 2 year of study, so N=8. Total variable costs include production and harvest costs, and production costs were based on standard desert conventional production costs adjusted to reflect actual costs for specific cover crops and mangement practices (but it implies the same costs were used for all replicates in a treatment so N=2) while harvest costs were adjusted to reflect variations in crop yield in each year (implying different costs were used per plot so N=8).
econ_inputs: production cost (border, cross check and breatk, fertilizer cost of application, transplant, install drip and repair, spike, hand weed, irrigation, insect control, land rent, cash overhead) ,harvesting
out_subindicator_description_raw: gross return was calculated as average price of $8/box (15.9kg) multiply by average boxes/ha for lettuce and $7.7/box (18kg) for cantaloupe. See Table 3 and 5 for breakdown of production costs. Harvest costs were adjusted based on number of boxes harvested per treatment multiplied by picking cost.
data_location: Economic output and yield from table 6 coordinates obtained from google

site_id01: The experimental trial was located in Sara Ana (390 m a.s.l.), Alto Beni, in the department of La Paz, Bolivia.
subpractice_description_raw: Afc: agroforestry conventional. the main shade trees were Inga spp. and Erythrina spp., complemented by timber, fruit, and }palm trees. In both the full-sun monocultures and agroforestry systems, plantain was also planted at a density of 625 trees ha?1. They were removed at the end of 2011 in the full-sun monocultures and replaced by banana trees in the agroforestry systems, according to local farmers practices.
out_exp_design: Each production system was replicated four times in a completely randomized block design.
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: Revenues derived from cacao were calculated for each  year, using the total dry yield per plot and annual average sales prices of each category of beans. The average weight of bunches necessary for a chipa was estimated }to calculate the revenues from the harvested bunches. Both the organic plantains and bananas were sold as conventional because of the lack of an organic market. The potential future revenues of the timber trees in the agroforestry systems were not considered because it was too early to estimate their potential growth at this stage and because we aimed to show the actual figures regarding the costs and benefits in a young cacao plantation. Costs: Pruning, weeding, and pest control
data_location: yield from figure 2 (Cacao 2014); economic outputs from figure 3 a and b

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri. Norther zone
subpractice_description_raw: PAS Norther zone: The PAS system was implemented during 2004 to 2014. Management practices used across the entire field included: (i) no-tillage; (ii) cover crops in all years; (iii) variable-rate N fertilizer applied to cereal grain crops using commercial ground-based canopy reflectance technologies (iv) zonal or variable-rate P, K and lime fertilizer based on 30-m gridsample soil-test results and University of Missouri fertilizer recommendations. In this zone, winter wheat replaced corn in PAS. Cover crops following wheat included medium red clover (Trifolium pratense L.), sudangrass (Sorghum sudanense P. Stapf) or mixtures of legumes and non-legumes.
chem_subpractice_raw: atrazine, other post-emerge plant-active herbicides, glyphosate
econ_inputs: cover crops, nitrogen, other fertilizers, tillage, pesticides,seed, harvest, shredding
out_subindicator_description_raw: Annual prices for inputs and outputs during 2007 to 2014 were considered in this analysis. Output prices for grain crops were obtained from the Center for Farm Financial Management (2018) for up to 2000 farms in nine Midwest states including Missouri. Average yields from replicated large plots adjacent to the field (Yost et al. 2016) with consistent management over time were utilized.
data_location: cost from table 4

site_id01: The study area was a 36 ha field in central Missouri. South zone

site_id01: ZIway
subpractice_description_raw: MD  SH: maize intercropped with sunnhemp and microdosed fertilizer
out_exp_design: Study conducted at three sites over 2 years (one cropping season per year), with three farmers per site each hosting one experimental block. So N = 3 farmers per study site x 3 subplots where yield was harvested x 2 years = 18
econ_inputs: seed, fertiliser, labour
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields were obtained through field measurements, while information on seeds, fertilisers and labour inputs were obtained through farmer interviews and field observations with costs obtained from local markets. Total revenue was based on sum of yields of maize, stover and legume biomass. Maize yields were based on harvesting crops from 70 sq m farm section covering three random 5.63 sq m of each subplot. The average of three subplots was taken and used to compute the yield per ha for each farm.
data_location: Table A1

site_id01: Maranhao state
subpractice_description_raw: SH: Small home garden: home garden <1 ha. Home gardens Multistrata agroforestry systems surround the houses, providing vital shade and omnipresent throughout our region. Dominant overstory trees are cupuassu, acai, cacao, banana, mango (Mangifera indica), and jackfruit (Artocarpus integrifolia), understory fruticulture and small domestic animals are further important components. Enriched fallow (EF) This agroforestry system is established by enrichment plantings of fruit and timber in the understory of old secondary forests. The degree of human control over vegetation structure and species composition is far lower than in all other systems covered by our study. Fallow enrichment developed independently in various site clusters by isolated initiatives of innovative farmers partly was initiated (and thereafter abandoned) by a local NGO. Enrichment plantings are an ecologically valuable option for income generation in otherwise unproductive forest reserves required by law. In our study, secondary forest age ranged from 6 to [30 years and enrichment planting age was 312 years. Banana, acai, and bacuri (Platonia insignis) are the most important enrichment species.
out_exp_design: Number of sites from figure 2 in parenthesis
econ_inputs: maintenance, harvest operations, external inputs and (own-family or hired) labor
out_subindicator_description_raw: semistructured interviews with open questions
data_location: the systems were classified as simplified or diversified according table 1 (number of species). Economic output from figure 5

site_id01: Ji County
subpractice_description_raw: Apple-peanut alley cropping system
out_exp_design: Experiment 1: 2005-2013. 3 replicates of each treatment arranged in blocks, but only the middle replicate was sampled. Each sampling plot was 160 sq m, with 3 tree rows and 2 alleyways in alley cropped systems, while each plot was 50 sq m for the monocropped systems. Sampling was done in the central region of each experiment block to avoid border effects. Experiment 2: 2014, but trees planted from first to ninth year after establishment and intercropping distance optimised. 3 replicates but only middle replicate sampled as in Exp. 1.
econ_inputs: labour(plantng and sowing, tending and harvesting
out_subindicator_description_raw: Yields were measured from all trees and crops in the sampling plots and weighted on an electronic scale
data_location: exp.2 cost tables 4,5 and 6

out_subindicator_description_raw: face-to-face questionnaires. These data were checked against the information available from cacao cooperatives (inputs purchase and cacao sale) and from other primary sources (technicians, other cooperative members, and cacao middlemen).
data_location: Economic outputs from table 1

site_id01: province of Guayas
subpractice_description_raw: Organic:  Farms with organic management are agroforestry systems with a high degree of diversification and no use of synthetic compounds, such as fertilizers or pesticides, and which have opted for the following: (1) technified irrigation and/or (2) the ecological and cultural intensification of management (high doses of organic fertilization, mechanic undergrowth control, cob collection, regular pruning for maintenance and formation, and the use of improved national varieties) with the purpose of also improving their yield per hectare.
econ_inputs: fertilization, crop protection, diesel and oil, electricity, tools, labor, water pump, sprinklers, pivot sprinklers
out_subindicator_description_raw: face-to-face questionnaires. These data were checked against the information available from cacao cooperatives (inputs purchase and cacao sale) and from other primary sources (technicians, other cooperative members, and cacao middlemen).
data_location: Economic outputs from table 1

site_id01: The present study was carried out in the villages of Govind Wildlife Sanctuary of hilly state Uttarakhand, Western Himalaya. Three villages, such as Jakhol, Sidiri, and Saoud, were selected after preliminary survey of the wildlife Sanctuary
subpractice_description_raw: Eleusine coracana  legume crops (Vigna mungo, Macrotyloma uniflorum, Glycine max)
out_exp_design: Three plots of each crop were randomly selected and all the inputs and outputs were quantified.
product_raw: economic yield, crop by product, green grasses
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: survey. All information was collected through informal discussions and individual interviews held during each cropping season for three successive years with knowledgeable members of the families particularly the women folk. Inputs like FYM, bullock hours, human labor hours and seeds, and outputs likes yield of grain, green grasses, and crop by-products were estimated for all the crops through field visits during different activities from field preparation to harvesting. Human labor was monitored in terms of hours devoted to different activities of agriculture operation by male and female farmers.
data_location: Economic outputs from table 2 (only Kharif season). Yield from figure 1. The article did not specify the meaning of the variances (I contacted them by email, he replied Standard deviation)

site_id01: Xinxiang Experimental Base of China Academy in agriculural sciences
subpractice_description_raw: W-M: wheat-maize
out_exp_design: Single plots for each treatment with an area of 70x4.8m, x 2 seasons per year x 2 years
econ_inputs: nd
out_subindicator_description_raw: Maize yields were measured following Ministry of Agriculture of China guidelines: The  yield of winter wheat was determined based on spike samples collected  from  double  lines  1  m  in  length. data_location: data from table 3, average across all seasons

site_id01: The cereal farming area under study is mainly situated in the southern part of the Kabul city
subpractice_description_raw: Cereals farm 2: wheat, clover, clover, clover, alfalfa, alfalfa, alfalfa, wheat, onion, wheat
econ_inputs: seeds, organic and inorganic fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation water, land leasing, hired labour charges, fuel, machinery needed for all field operations
out_subindicator_description_raw: All input prices and the farm gate price of each type of produce sold were recorded. Costs of manure and irrigation water were calculated based on current prices at the farm gate. All expenses and revenues were computed on a hectare basis and converted from the local currency Afghani to US$ at a rate of 50:1. Depreciation of irrigation equipment as well as interest on capital for the variable expenses incurred by the farmers during the crop season was not taken into account in our calculations of production costs. Furthermore the establishment costs of the vineyard plantations were considered amortized over their past 40 years life span. Therefore, no further amortization costs were considered.
data_location: economic outcomes from table 4

site_id01: A field experiment was conducted between July until December 2017 at Mungka, Lima Puluh Kota Regency, West Sumatra province in Indonesia.
subpractice_description_raw: ORB:organic rice with border crops. The ORB system combined organic rice cultivation with the additional border crops sunn hemp (Crotalaria juncea) and long beans (Phaseolus vulgaris) grown on the dikes.
chem_subpractice_raw: extracts of garlic (Allium sativum) and ginger (Zingiber officinale) and wood ash and salt were used to control pests and diseases in these systems
out_exp_design: The experimental design was a Randomised Complete Block Design with six treatments and three replicate blocks.
econ_inputs: NA
out_subindicator_description_raw: The economic performance of each treatment was assessed from the input costs and the revenue of the harvested products of one rice cropping cycle. Besides producing rice, the treatments ORB, ORF, ORD, and CRS also produced long beans, fish and/or ducks. The investment and potential revenue of these marketable products were included in the economic calculations. Input costs consisted of costs for fertilisers (kg), pesticides (kg), compost (kg), bio-pesticides (packet), plant seeds (kg), long bean seeds (kg), ducks, fish, duck foods (kg), fish foods (kg) and labour (hours). The costs of labour were determined from the actual labour input in the experiment and were calculated based on farm wage per hour in Indonesian Rupiah (IDR). The revenue from the harvested rice (kg), long beans (kg), fish (kg), and ducks (birds) was calculated based on production and prices. The price was determined by the market price for the product at the time of the experiment. The gross margin per treatment was  calculated as the difference between the revenue and costs. Finally,
data_location: yield from table 3. Economic output from table 4. Labor input from figure 2

site_id01: This research was conducted from 1992 through 1994 in Huron County, Michigan.
subpractice_description_raw: Red clover system (0)
out_exp_design: 4 replications per treatment x 2 years = 8 sample size
econ_inputs: cost of red clover seed, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system.
out_subindicator_description_raw: The net income of each cropping system was calculated by subtracting selected variable costs from gross income. Selected variable costs included the cost of red clover seed, N fertilizer, fertilizer application, and operation, maintenance, and depreciation of the SI system. Gross income was calculated by multiplying corn grain yield from each treatment by the 1993 government target price for corn grain ($2.50). The costs of N fertilizer and fertilizer application were obtained from the Huron County, MICHIGAN, Cooperative Extension Service. Hauling and drying costs are not included. Depreciation cost was calculated by dividing the $3OO/acre cost of installing the SI system by the 20 yr expected life of the system. Installation, maintenance, and operation costs, and expected life of the SI system are fiom Belcher (1992).
data_location: gross and net income from table 8
